coredata.dc:title: Comparative life cycle assessment of bottom-up synthesis routes for carbon dots derived from citric acid and urea
coredata.dc:creator: Sendão, Ricardo.
Yuso, Maria del Valle Martínez de
Algarra, Manuel
Esteves da Silva, Joaquim C.G.
Pinto da Silva, Luís
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanoparticles with remarkable luminescent properties, which have made them exciting and suitable alternatives to more traditional fluorophores and even to more recent luminescent nanomaterials (such as metal-based quantum dots). However, despite this high interest on CDs, there has been no focus on their sustainable development and fabrication, and so, there is lacking concrete data on their environmental impacts. A life cycle assessment (LCA) approach was used here to compare and understand the environmental impacts of carbon dots (CDs) obtained via six representative bottom-up synthetic strategies (cradle-to-gate). These routes consist on hydrothermal and microwave-assisted synthesis of CDs derived of citric acid (with the occasional addition of urea), which represent current trends in the synthesis of CDs. Results show that for hydrothermal synthesis the use of electricity is dominant for almost all environmental categories, while citric acid produces most impacts for microwave-assisted synthesis. A performance-based comparison was also made by rescaling results with the fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs. This approach changed the rank order of preference in all categories by a significant margin. While previous analysis indicated microwave-assisted synthesis of citric acid-derived CDs to be the most benign in environmental terms, now the option is the synthesis (either by hydrothermal or microwave-assisted treatment) of urea and citric acid-derived CDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Block Flow Diagram for the six routes of fabrication of CDs.

Relative environmental impacts of hydrothermally-synthesized CDs with the ReCiPe2016 LCIA: CA-based CDs at 200 °C for 2 h (A); CA-based CDs at 200 °C for 4 h (B); CA,urea-based CDs at 200 °C for 2 h (C). Abbreviations are explained under Section 2.6.

Relative environmental impacts of microwave-assisted-synthesized CDs with the ReCiPe2016 LCIA: CA-based CDs under microwave irradiation for 5 min (A); CA-based CDs under microwave irradiation for 10 min (B); CA,urea-based CDs under microwave irradiation for 5 min (C). The abbreviations are the same as in Fig. 1.

Comparative environmental (A and B) profiles for the six different routes for the synthesis of CDs. Environmental profiles were obtained using ReCiPe 2016 v1.1 as a LCIA. Graphic A refers to the LCA analysis using a volume-based functional unit of 1 L of CDs, while Graphic B is re-scaled with respect to the QYFL of the CDs. Synthesis: hydrothermal treatment of CA and urea at 200 °C for 2 h (dark green); hydrothermal treatment of CA at 200 °C for 2 h (light green); hydrothermal treatment of CA at 200 °C for 4 h (orange); microwave-assisted synthesis of CA,urea-based CDs with 5 min irradiation (yellow); microwave-assisted synthesis of CA-based CDs with 5 min irradiation (dark blue); microwave-assisted synthesis of CA-based CDs with 10 min irradiation (light blue). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Fluorescence quantum yield (QYFL) and QYFL-normalized functional unit (QYFL-FU) for the synthesized CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanoparticles with remarkable luminescent properties, which have made them exciting and suitable alternatives to more traditional fluorophores and even to more recent luminescent nanomaterials (such as metal-based quantum dots). However, despite this high interest on CDs, there has been no focus on their sustainable development and fabrication, and so, there is lacking concrete data on their environmental impacts. A life cycle assessment (LCA) approach was used here to compare and understand the environmental impacts of carbon dots (CDs) obtained via six representative bottom-up synthetic strategies (cradle-to-gate). These routes consist on hydrothermal and microwave-assisted synthesis of CDs derived of citric acid (with the occasional addition of urea), which represent current trends in the synthesis of CDs. Results show that for hydrothermal synthesis the use of electricity is dominant for almost all environmental categories, while citric acid produces most impacts for microwave-assisted synthesis. A performance-based comparison was also made by rescaling results with the fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs. This approach changed the rank order of preference in all categories by a significant margin. While previous analysis indicated microwave-assisted synthesis of citric acid-derived CDs to be the most benign in environmental terms, now the option is the synthesis (either by hydrothermal or microwave-assisted treatment) of urea and citric acid-derived CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) are defined as purposely developed manufactured materials with dimensions between 1 nm and 100 nm (Auffan et al., 2009; Cattaneo et al., 2010). Furthermore, ENMs have different properties than the bulk materials with the same composition (Cattaneo et al., 2010; Feijoo et al., 2017). Despite the increase in the commercial production volumes of ENMs, it is still unclear what are the potential environmental impacts resulting from their production and use (Fadeel et al., 2018; Salieri et al., 2018). Thus, a detailed understanding of the impacts made by ENMs during their life cycle is essential in order to guarantee their sustainable production and usage. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is the most appropriated tool for solving this problem (Hischier and Walser, 2012; Upadhyayula et al., 2012), as it aims to quantify the environmental impacts of a given system during its life cycle (Hauschild, 2005; Ramos et al., 2018a, 2018b).

In fact, this tool has already been used in the environmental evaluation of different ENMs: carbon nanotubes (Upadhyayula et al., 2012), silver nanoparticles (Pourzahedi and Eckelman, 2015; Bafana et al., 2018), magnetic nanoparticles (Feijoo et al., 2017), graphene oxide (Deng et al., 2017), copper nanoparticles (Pu et al., 2016), and TiO2 nanoparticles (Ettrup et al., 2017), among others.

However, there has not been any study focusing the environmental evaluation of a important and specific ENM: carbon dots (CDs). These are a class of emerging carbon-based spherical nanoparticles, typically sized between 1 and 10 nm (Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Wang et al., 2017). CDs have attracted significant attention due to their good water solubility (Xiong et al., 2018), strong photoluminescence (Wang et al., 2017; Xiong et al., 2018), broadband optical absorption (Peng et al., 2017), biocompatibility (Liu et al., 2009) and low toxicity (Yang et al., 2009). CDs are also easy to synthesize and functionalize (Wang and Hu, 2014; Shamsipur et al., 2017). Given this, CDs have been used as photoluminescent materials in several applications, such as: bioimaging (Feng et al., 2016; Zhi et al., 2018), photocatalysis (Wang et al., 2017), light-emitting devices (Zhou et al., 2018a,b), nanothermometry (Kalytchuk et al., 2017), optoelectronic devices (Tetsuka et al., 2016; Arcudi et al., 2017), and sensing (Crista et al., 2019; Campos et al., 2017, 2016).

Despite this, there has been no particular focus on their sustainable development and fabrication. Thus, providing information regarding the potential environmental impacts of representative synthesis strategies for CDs’ production is significant and relevant.

CDs can be synthesized via two main classes: top-down and bottom-up (Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Wang et al., 2017). The former approach consists on the breakdown of large graphite materials into smaller carbon-based materials (Zhou et al., 2018a,b; Gonçalves et al., 2010). However, the most widely used synthesis method appears to be the preparation of CDs from citric acid (CA) as carbon source (Xiong et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018a,b), many times with addition of amine-containing molecules as nitrogen source (Sendão et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2018a,b), via bottom-up methods such as hydrothermal treatment (Cailotto et al., 2018; Feng et al., 2018; Simões et al., 2020) and microwave-assisted (Shen et al., 2018; Nemati et al., 2018; Sri et al., 2018) methods. However, there is a lack of data regarding the potential environmental impacts generated by these synthetic strategies. This is problematic since the production steps of ENMs have already been identified as of being an environmental concern (Pourzahedi and Eckelman, 2015), as production of ENMs can be orders of magnitude more energy- and material consuming than for fine chemicals and pharmaceuticals (Eckelman et al., 2008). Furthermore, LCA studies of ENMs have shown that the energy and chemicals used during the synthesis of these nanomaterials can contribute a significant share of the environmental impacts generated during their life cycle (Essner et al., 2018; Huijbregts et al., 2017).

Herein, the aim of this work is to evaluate the environmental impacts associated with the synthesis of CDs using an LCA approach. To this end, it will be considered two types of bottom-up strategies: hydrothermal and microwave-assisted synthesis. Moreover, CA will be the main carbon-source with urea sometimes added as a nitrogen-source. More specifically, the hydrothermal synthesis will be as follow: (i) CA treated hydrothermally at 200 °C for 2 h; (ii) CA treated hydrothermally at 200 °C for 4 h; (iii) CA and urea treated hydrothermally at 200 °C for 2 h. The microwave-assisted synthesis is as follow: (iv) aqueous solution of CA under microwave irradiation for 5 min; (v) aqueous solution of CA under microwave irradiation for 10 min; (vi) aqueous solution of CA and urea under microwave irradiation for 5 min. It should be highlighted that these synthesis classes (hydrothermal and microwave-assisted) are the arguably the most widely used strategies for fabrication of CDs (Cailotto et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018; Nemati et al., 2018). Thus, the conclusions obtained in this study are not limited to these specific syntheses but can be applied to the fabrication of other CDs. In fact, CA is one of the most common carbon sources in the synthesis of CDs (Xiong et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018a,b).

While the initial stage of the LCA study will apply a simplified functional unit (Feijoo et al., 2017; Pourzahedi and Eckelman, 2015), it will also be considered a functional unit more related with an improved functionality of the CDs: the fluorescent quantum yield (QYFL). Such approach is essential given that a more resource-intensive synthesis may be justified latter in the use stage.

The experimental section is divided into seven sub-sections: synthesis procedures for CDs (2.1); fluorescence characterization of CDs (2.2); HPLC and XPS characterization of the CDs (2.3); scope and system boundaries (2.4); life cycle inventory data (2.5); details relative to environmental impact assessment (2.6); sensitivity analysis (2.7).

The detailed procedure can be found in the Supporting Information. Briefly, the CDs were prepared by six different routes. The amounts are related to a final volume of 1 L of CDs. Three routes were based on the hydrothermal treatment of CA for either 2 or 4 h at 200 °C, with one of them involving the addition of urea. The other three routes were based on the microwave treatment of CA for either 5 or 10 min, with one of them also involving the addition of urea.

All synthesized CDs were subsequently centrifuged (in reusable centrifuge tubes) at 6000 rpm during 20 min in order to eliminate suspended impurities, by using a MIKRO 220 R centrifuge from Hettich. Centrifugation is a common and simple purification step in the synthesis of CDs (Crista et al., 2019; Simões et al., 2016, 2017).

Fluorescence spectra were obtained in standard 10 mm fluorescence quartz cells and collected in a Horiba Jovin Yvon Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorimeter. Absorbance measurements were made with a UNICAM Helios Gamma, using quartz cells. The QYFL was calculated by comparing the integrated luminescence intensities and absorbance values of the synthesized CDs with the ones of quinine sulphate, with the following equation:

In equation (1), Grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance and η is the refractive index. Quinine sulphate was chosen as a reference fluorophore of known quantum yield (QYFL = 0.54) (Melhuish, 1961).

ce:section.ce:section-title: HPLC and XPS characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The RP HPLC-DAD chromatographic system consisted of a Thermo Scientific SpectraSystem P1000 pump, a Rheodyne manual injection valve, a SUPELCOSIL™ LC-18 column and a Thermo Finnigan UV6000 LP diode array detector. It was used a mobile phase made of 30% acetonitrile and 70% of an 1% ammonium acetate volume. The flow was set to 0.80 mL per minute.

X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were recorded on a Physical Electronic PHI VersaProbe II spectrometer utilizing Al-Kα monochromatic radiation with a hemispherical multichannel detector (53.6 W, 15 kV and 1486.6 eV). Spectra were recorded using a 200 μm diameter circular analysis area, with a constant pass energy value at 29.35 eV. PHI SmartSoft software was employed for results analysis, further processed using MultiPak Version 9.6 package. Carbon C 1s signal (284.8 eV) was used as reference to determine the binding energy values, using Shirley-type background and Gauss-Lorentz curves.

The aim of this cradle-to-gate study is to assess the potential environmental impacts of different routes for the bottom-up synthesis of new CDs, which is focused on the laboratory-scale manufacturing stage of the target ENMs and considers the direct emissions from CDs’ production and indirect impacts associated with upstream resource extraction and energy generation.

This work uses three hydrothermal and three microwave-assisted synthetic strategies. The production schemes were designed by our group, and the detailed description of the six routes can be found in the Experimental Section (Fig. 1 ). The environmental impacts were analyzed and compared in a first stage using a volume-based functional unit of 1 L of the aqueous solution of CDs. This was made because the selection of a weight or volume based functional unit makes sense to compare the production of an equivalent amount of nanomaterial (Feijoo et al., 2017; Hischier and Walser, 2012). However, these impacts were normalized by the QYFL of the different CDs, in a later stage. Such approach is needed because weight-/volume-based functional units do not consider functional benefits of ENMs for which they were engineered, and so, they might not account for the possibility that a more resource-intensive synthesis may be justified latter in the use stage (given the improved functionality). The QYFL was chosen as the normalization factor because while CDs have many different applications, a high QYFL is generally a requested/desired property in most, if not all, of these applications.

The assessment of the environmental impacts was based on inventory (foreground) data from the laboratory-scale synthesis procedures described on section 2.1 and on the Supporting Information. The life cycle inventory data for the foreground system consisted on average data found in the Ecoinvent ® 3.4 database for the synthesis procedures (primary data).

The synthesis procedure of the CDs consists on the hydrothermal (by using an oven) or microwave-based (by using a domestic microwave) treatment of an aqueous solution of the precursors for a given time, followed by purification via a centrifugation step. Therefore, the foreground system consists only on the production of chemicals (CA, urea and deionized water) used as raw materials for the fabrication of the CDs, and the generation of the electricity used for the synthesis process and the purification step (by centrifugation).

The different processes and chemicals included in this study were modelled with the following data present in the Ecoinvent ® 3.4 database: CA - citric acid {GLO} | market for; Urea – Urea, as N {GLO} | market for; Deionized water - Water, deionized, from tap water, at user {Europe without Switzerland} | market for; Electricity – Electricity, medium voltage {PT} | market for. The dataset used for electricity describes the available electricity data available on the medium voltage level in Portugal for year 2014, as described in the Ecoinvent ® 3.4 database.

As referred above, the electricity considered combines the electricity required for using the centrifuge and either an oven (hydrothermal treatment) or a domestic microwave (microwave-assisted synthesis). Centrifugation was made by using a MIKRO 220 R centrifuge (from Hettich), which has a power supply of 230 V with a frequency of 50 Hz. Hydrothermal synthesis was made in a VWR DL 112 Prime oven, which possesses a capacity of 112 L and a nominal power consumption of 2500 W. Microwave-assisted synthesis was made in an Electronia domestic microwave (model P70B17L-DE), with a power consumption of 700 W.

The amounts of CA, urea and deionized water used are: 100 g of CA, 100 g of urea and 2 L of deionized water. The amounts of electricity used are: 5.29 kWh for a hydrothermal treatment with a duration of 2 h; 10.29 kWh for a hydrothermal treatment with a duration of 4 h; 0.35 kWh for a microwave-assisted synthesis with an irradiation duration of 5 min; 0.42 kWh for a microwave-assisted synthesis with an irradiation duration of 10 min.

The present LCA study is based on a cradle-to-gate approach, from the production of precursor materials to the fabrication of CDs. Environmental impacts were modelled using the ReCiPe 2016 v1.1 LCIA, Hierarchist version (Huijbregts et al., 2017). The impact potentials evaluated according to the ReCiPe method were: global warming - human health (GW – HH), global warming - terrestrial ecosystems (GW – TE), global warming - freshwater ecosystems (GW - FE), stratospheric ozone depletion (SO), ionization radiation (IR), ozone formation - human health (OF – HH), fine particulate matter formation (FPM), ozone formation - terrestrial ecosystems (OF – TE), terrestrial acidification (TA), freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutrophication (ME), terrestrial ecotoxicity (TE), freshwater ecotoxicity (FET), marine ecotoxicity (MET), human carcinogenic toxicity (HC), human non-carcinogenic toxicity (HNC), land use (LU), mineral resource scarcity (MR), fossil resource scarcity (FR), water consumption - human health (WC – HH), water consumption - terrestrial ecosystem (WC – TE) and water consumption - aquatic ecosystems (WC – AE). The LCA study was performed by using the SimaPro 8.5.2.0 software.

Given that CDs are still in early stages of technological development at laboratory scale, the assessment of environmental impacts in their fabrication is still uncertain. This, it was performed a sensitivity analysis in order to help to determine such uncertainties for scale-up production (Kazemi et al., 2018). This was made by considering “What-if” scenarios (Pianosi et al., 2016), that is, by varying the amount and type of materials and electricity employed in the synthesis of the CDs.
The Results and Discussion section is divided into two sub-sections: fluorescence, purity and morphological characterization of the CDs (3.1); LCA-based evaluation of their synthesis procedure (3.2).

In Fig. S1 are presented the fluorescence spectra of the six new CDs in aqueous solution, while their QYFL are presented in Table 1 . Increasing the duration of microwave irradiation seems to have a slight impact on the photoluminescence of the CDs, given that the fluorescence wavelength maxima suffers a ∼27 nm blue-shift when the irradiation time is doubled. However, the QYFL (∼6%) of microwave-obtained CA-based CDs are similar. Increasing the duration of the heating period for the hydrothermal approach also does not have a relevant effect on the QYFL, given that this parameter is quite similar for hydrothermal-obtained CA-based CDs (∼8%). However, this is not true for the fluorescence wavelength maximum, given that doubling the heating period of the hydrothermal approach led to a slight ∼21 nm blue-shift (∼461 nm at 2 h versus ∼440 nm at 4 h). Another conclusion of interest is that addition of urea leads to a very significant increase in the QYFL of the CDs, irrespective of CD being synthesized by a microwave-assisted (∼52%) or by a hydrothermal approach (∼49%). However, addition of urea to the microwave-assisted synthesis leads to a ∼50 nm red-shift in the emission (when comparing with the CA-based CD obtained at the same irradiation duration), while addition of urea to the hydrothermal procedure leads to ∼6 nm blue-shift.

Having performed their photoluminescent characterization, the next step was to assess the purity, and perform structural characterization of the CDs with highest QYFL: microwave-assisted and hydrothermally-treated CA,urea-based CDs (Table 1). The purity of the hydrothermally and microwave-assisted CA,urea-based CDs was assessed by analysis with a reverse-phase (RP) HPLC coupled to a diode array detector (RP-HPLC-DAD), with the chromatograms present in Fig. S2. The chromatogram of the microwave-assisted CA,urea-based CDs is composed by multiple peaks, which indicates the presence of different composites produced from the microwave treatment of the CDs. The possibility that these aqueous CDs’ solutions are composed by mixtures of different particles indicates that their purity might not be satisfactory. Thus, further steps of more complex, energy- and resources-demanding purification (such as dialysis, electrophoretic or chromatographic separation) may be required (Essner et al., 2018). For the contrary, the chromatogram of the hydrothermally-produced CA,urea-based CDs is composed by a single peak, which might indicate that their purity should be satisfactory and that further purification steps may be avoided.

To understand the surface composition and electronic states of the constituent elements present in the obtained CA,urea-based CDs, XPS analysis was carried out as shown in Fig. S3 (Campos et al., 2015). The content (in %) of C, N and O on the surface of CDs is 59.70, 16.75 and 23.55%, respectively, after microwave treatment for 5 min. By its turn, hydrothermal treatment led to a C, N and O content of 55.74, 14.06 and 30.20%, respectively. These results show a decrease of the C and N contents with an increase of O content when comparing microwave-irradiation with hydrothermal treatment, which is explained by the oxidation process carried out on the surface at 200 °C. Given that the survey scan of the obtained CDs revealed the peaks of three elements (C, N and O), we performed a detailed scan for the C 1s, O 1s and N 1s internal levels for deconvolution and chemical state and the quantitative analysis. The C 1s of both CDs further splits into three peaks: at binding energies 284.8 (54.53%), 286.6 (11.95%) and 288.3 eV (33.5%) (Fig. S3A), respectively, for the CD obtained from microwave-irradiation; at binding energies 284.8 (54.79%), 286.9 (17.1%) and 288.6 eV (28.1%), respectively, when treated hydrothermally (Fig. S3D). These peaks are attributed to C–C/C–H/adventitious carbon (284.8 eV), C–O/C–N (286.6–286.9 eV) and CO/O–CO carbonyl/carboxylic groups (288.3–288.6 eV), respectively. This indicates that when comparison microwave-irradiation with hydrothermal treatment, there is a decrease of the CO/O–CO contribution with an increase of the C–O/C–N contribution.

The deconvolution of O 1s spectrum yielded a major peak at 531.5 eV (80.9%) due to (carbonyl) CO linkage, with a relatively small contribution at 532.7 eV (18.1%), which was ascribed to C–O/C–O–C group, for the CDs formed via microwave-irradiation (Fig. S3B). A similar profile was found for the CA,urea-based CDs treated hydrothermally, but with an increased C–O contribution up to 32.42% (Fig. S3E).

For N 1s core level spectra were found a dominant peak 400 eV (88.9%) which is attributed to amine/amide groups. In addition, a shoulder was noticed at 401.5 eV (11.1%), which is attributed to protonated amine. These % of contributions were found in samples treated with microwave-irradiation (Fig. S3C). When treated hydrothermally, the higher binding energy contribution increases to 30.5%, explained by the incorporation of the protonated amine to the surface of the CDs (Fig. S3F). As can be observed from the N 1s spectra, the ratio between bands (400/401.5) changes from 8.03 (microwave-irradiation) to 2.3 (hydrothermal treatment), justifying the presence of amine groups on the CDs surface after hydrothermal reaction.

In conclusion, the chromatographic analysis that microwave-assisted CA-urea-based CDs should require further steps of more complex purification (Essner et al., 2018). Besides this leading to more consumption of energy and resources, purification of CDs has been associated with a decrease of QYFL given that the eliminated by-products tend to contribute positively of the fluorescence of these ENMs (Essner et al., 2018). Thus, these aspects indicate that the hydrothermal preparation of CA,urea-based CDs should be preferable to microwave-assisted synthesis.

This sub-section is divided into the assessment of environmental impacts associated either with a volume-based functional unit (3.2.1) or a function-based functional unit (3.2.2), on sensitivity evaluation (3.2.3), and on scale-up modelling (3.2.4).

It should be noted that our aim is to compare the contributions to the different impact categories of the input materials involved in each synthesis with each other, and not make quantitative appreciations of the environmental impacts of each input. Analysis of the hydrothermal synthesis showed that electricity is the resource that shows the higher contribution, when compared with other resources, to most environmental impacts. The exceptions are the categories of stratospheric ozone depletion, marine eutrophication, land use and water consumption, in which CA has the highest contributions. The addition of urea only results in relatively small contributions (when compared with the other resources), except for terrestrial ecotoxicity, mineral and fossil resource scarcity and water consumption. Deionized water does not appear to have any relevant environmental impact, when comparing with the other resources, even in the categories related with water consumption.

Interestingly, the results for microwave-assisted synthesis (Fig. 3) are quite the opposite from those obtained for the hydrothermal-based routes (Fig. 2). Namely, the use of electricity leads to relatively small impacts, when comparing with other used resources, which is not unexpected given that it only results from a 5- or 10-min irradiation. Given this, CA is the main contributor to all the environmental impact categories. It should be noted that given the comparatively reduced contribution of electricity, the relative contribution of urea is now quite significant. In fact, urea has contributions as high or even higher than CA in some categories, such as terrestrial ecotoxicity and fossil resource scarcity. Deionized water still presents a comparatively negligible contribution.

To facilitate the comparison between the six different synthetic routes, they are compared in the same Figure (Fig. 4 A), with all environmental impact categories being divided into three main categories: human health, ecosystems and resources. In summary, the synthesis of the CDs by hydrothermal treatment generally leads to higher environmental impacts than by using a microwave-assisted approach. Moreover, doubling the duration of the hydrothermal treatment, from 2 to 4 h, leads to a relevant increase in the resulting environmental impacts in all categories. Finally, addition of urea as a precursor leads only to a relatively small effect in terms of human health and ecosystems. However, this is not the case for the environmental impacts related to resource usage. More specifically, addition of urea within the hydrothermal approach has almost the same effect as doubling the duration of the synthesis (2–4 h). Also, the addition of urea during the microwave-assisted synthesis results in a 5-min synthesis having almost the same environmental impact in terms of resources than a 2-h synthesis.

It was also compared the environmental profiles of the six different synthetic routes, when rescaled with respect to the QYFL of the resulting CDs (Fig. 4B). This re-scaling was made by considering the highest QYFL (that of microwave-derived CA,urea-based CD, of 51.98%) as the reference QYFL (
Q
Y
F L
R E F
). The QYFL – normalized functional unit for each CD was then calculated as
Q
Y
F L

(Table 1).

The results did not change much with the rescaling, given that hydrothermal treatment still produces CDs with significantly higher environmental impacts than microwave-assisted synthesis. Thus, hydrothermally produced CA-based CDs should be the worst option irrespective of the environmental impacts considered. The main differences are, however, related with the CDs where urea was added as a nitrogen-source. When volume was the chosen functional unit (Fig. 4A), the synthesis of CA,urea-based CDs were the second (hydrothermal treatment) and fourth (microwave-assisted synthesis) worst options regarding synthetic routes, irrespective of the environmental categories considered. The inclusion of QYFL as a factor in the analysis led to significant changes making these CDs as the first (microwave-assisted) and second (hydrothermal treatment) best options for synthetic routes in terms of potential environmental impacts.

In summary, CA,urea-based CDs are the best option for the synthetic route in terms of environmental impact, when QYFL was included in the analysis and irrespective of being a hydrothermal or microwave-assisted approach. This was not the case when just a volume-based functional unit was used, which demonstrates the need to include the performance of the studied ENMs in the LCA study. Furthermore, the present results show that hydrothermal treatment generally produces significantly higher environmental impacts than corresponding microwave-assisted synthesis. Therefore, the former approach should be considered as a worst option for a synthetic route, while the latter should generally be favored.

A sensitivity analysis was performed by focusing on the two CDs, which synthesis led to lower environmental impacts when considering the QYFL: microwave-derived and hydrothermal-based CA,urea-based CDs. These scenarios consist on changing the input of the electricity and raw materials (CA and urea) by ±30%, and on changing the identity of the precursors. More specifically, changing the carbon source from CA to glucose, and the nitrogen source from urea to ethylenediamine (EDA). Both glucose (Liu et al., 2018) and EDA (Simões et al., 2015) are also common precursors for the synthesis of CDs.

The results for hydrothermal-based CA,urea-based CDs can be found on Figures S5, S6 and S7. Changing the amount of urea (Figure S5A) has a negligible effect on the human health and ecosystem categories (±3%), with a more moderate effect on resources (±8%). Given this, it is not surprising that replacing urea by EDA (Fig. S6) only resulted in significant changes in the resources categories (increased impact by 16%), while this replacement only led to ∼3% variations for other categories. Changing the amount of CA (Figure S5B) also only has a moderate effect (±6% in all three categories). However, replacing CA by glucose as a carbon source (Fig. S7) led to 15% decrease in all impact categories. Finally, varying the electricity input (Figure S5C) also results in more significant alterations in the environmental impacts. In fact, the impact of human health and ecosystems vary by ±16%, while the resources categories vary by ±13%. Thus, both electricity and CA appear to be important sensitive factors to influence the environmental impacts of the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs.

For the contrary, varying the electricity input (Figure S8C) has only an almost negligible effect (±3%) on the three environmental impact categories, for microwave-derived CA,urea-based CDs. By its turn, varying the amount of CA (Figure S8B) led to more significant variations (between ±10% and ±16%). Following this trend, replacing CA by glucose (Fig. S10) led to remarkable decreases in the environmental impact relative to the human health (44%), ecosystems (33%) and resources (27%) categories. Lastly, varying the amount of urea (Figure S8A) also led to moderate changes in the human health (±8%) and ecosystems (±7%) categories. More significant were the changes relative to the resources category (±14%). Lastly, replacing urea by EDA (Fig. S9) led to a quite significant increase of environmental impacts relative to the resources categories (26%), while the increases associated with the other categories were more moderate (6%). In summary, for the microwave-assisted synthesis of CDs, the use of CA appears to be the critical point in terms of environmental impacts, with urea also being a sensitive factor in terms of the resources category.

However, it should be pointed out that the QYFL of a given nanomaterial is dependent on its structure, which in turn is dependent on the identity and ratio of the used raw material. Thus, while the sensitivity analysis showed that CA, and urea up to a point, are quite sensitive factors regarding the environmental impacts, more study is required before it can be recommended the variation of their inputs, or even their replacement altogether. That is, because changes in these parameters will likely modify the resulting QYFL, which could offset the expected environmental benefits.

To obtain further information regarding the development of CDs on a real scale, it was extrapolated the laboratory scale results of hydrothermal CA,urea-based CDs to industrial scale, by considering the framework developed by Piccinno et al. (2016). Under this framework, the target process takes place in a batch reactor with a volume between 100 and 10,000 l (the processing quantity). Chosen the processing quantity, the amount of reactants used (CA and urea) are scaled linearly up to that volume. To maintain the concentration of the reactants, the quantity of water used is also scale-up linearly. The framework developed by Piccinno et al. (2016) also determines how the energy required for the heating and centrifugation steps are calculated. Namely, the heating energy required for the hydrothermal treatment (Qreact) is calculated by using Equation (1) (Piccinno et al., 2016), in which C p is the specific heat capacity, m mix the mass of the reaction mixture, T

a the thermal conductivity of the insulation material, s the thickness of the insulation, t the time of the reaction, and η heat the efficiency of the heating device.

By its turn, the energy requirement for the centrifugation step was calculated by considering the estimate of 10 kWh/ton suggested by Piccinno et al. (2016). The duration of the reaction (2 h) and the reaction temperature (200 °C) remain the same as in at the laboratory scale. The involved material in- and output quantities can be found on Table S1.

First, it was compared the single score contribution (in %) of the input materials between industrial (using a processing quantity of 100 l) and laboratory scale (Figure S11A). As referred before, at the laboratory scale the synthesis of this type of CD is impacted quite significantly by the electricity (66.5%), followed by CA (22.2%). Urea only presents as small contribution (11.1%), while the contribution by water is negligible (0.1%). This scenario is quite different at the industrial scale. In fact, the highest impact is now from CA (58.8%), followed by urea (29.4%). As for electricity, its contribution is now quite small (11.4%). Thus, the present extrapolation indicates that at the industrial scale, it is the precursors (and not the used electricity) to be the environmental critical point in the synthesis of CDs. It should be noted that the single score contribution (in %) of input materials with a processing quantity of 1000 l is quite similar to those presented by input materials with a processing quantity of 100 l (Figure S11A). Thus, changing the volume of the batch reactor does not appear to change the relative contributions in percentage of each input material to the global impact of each synthesis.

In fact, analysis of the environmental profile for this synthesis at industrial scale (Figure S11B) reveals that CA has the highest impacts in the human health and ecosystem (58.7% and 64.5%, respectively) categories, while presenting the second highest impact on the resources category (38.5%). By its turn, urea is the highest contributor the resources category (55.5%), and the second highest one for the human health and ecosystems (29.2% and 25.5%, respectively) categories. As for electricity, its impacts are only between 5.9% and 11.7% for the three categories. Water only has negligible impacts.

This work provides the first detailed LCA-based environmental assessment for representative synthesis strategies of carbon dots (CDs). The studied routes include bottom-up strategies such as hydrothermal and microwave-assisted treatment of aqueous solutions of citric acid (CA) (as a carbon-source) and occasionally of urea (as a nitrogen-source), arguably the most common and relevant synthetic schemes for CDs.

The simple analysis of the synthesis performance by volume of CDs indicated that the most sustainable option is microwave-assisted treatment of aqueous solutions of CA, which can be attributed to two parameters: lower electricity usage and absence of urea. However, the recommendation of this option is prevented by its low performance in terms of QYFL. When considering a QYFL-based performance, the most reliable option is the use of an aqueous solution of CA and urea as raw material followed by either microwave or hydrothermal treatment, given that the high QYFL of the resulting CDs offset the higher environmental impacts associated with these synthesis routes. In general, the present data indicated that the use of electricity is the main contributor to most environmental categories in hydrothermal synthesis, while CA is the major contributor for the microwave-assisted synthesis.

A sensitivity analysis was also performed to evaluate how the environmental impact of the synthesis of CDs is affected by variations in raw material and electricity input. For hydrothermal-based synthesis, electricity is the most sensitive parameter regarding changes in the amount used, while varying the amount of CA and urea does not cause significant alterations. However, the replacement of CA by other carbon source (glucose) reduces significantly the environmental impacts associated with this synthesis. The replacement of CA for glucose leads to even more significant reductions of the environmental impacts for microwave-assisted synthesis. Urea is also a sensitive parameter, but its replacement by another common nitrogen source increased the associated environmental impacts. By its turn, electricity is not a sensitive parameter for microwave-assisted synthesis.
Scale-up to industrial scale of hydrothermal synthesis of CA,urea-based CDs revealed that the critical points in terms of environmental impacts are the precursors (specially CA), and not electricity.

In summary, the benefits obtained by nitrogen-doping strategies relative to the QYFL performance of CDs offset the environmental impacts associated with their use, for both bottom-up approaches here used. Moreover, it was found that only the carbon source used as a raw material is a critical point in terms of environmental impacts for both synthesis strategies, and so, should become a focus on future studies regarding the cleaner production of CDs.
